title: Database schema
text: ttr.db is a SQLite database built by scripts/build_db.py from the campaign workbook and the replay-PDF reference. Stored tables hold facts only; all arithmetic lives in views (scripts/views.sql).

title: Database schema
text:
Key concepts

run_id — the backtracked unstacking order. The workbook’s id column is one global sequence shared by tickets, employees, events, post-office and story cards: the order the dead letter office pile was taken apart. run_id = 0 in the source marks cards found while going through a player’s vault (no pile position); those are stored with run_id NULL. Story pause/stop cards carry game years, so any card’s run position between two dated markers bounds when it was retired.
card_uid — the proper legacy card code (GP-14, CS-08, …). East Coast starter tickets carry no printed number, so they get synthesized codes EC-01..EC-33 (alphabetical by route).

title: Database schema
text:
Entity relationships





erDiagram
    players ||--o{ ticket_punches : "punches"
    players ||--o{ circus : "flyer rows"
    players ||--o{ timetable_cells : "cells"
    players ||--o{ bank_slips : "scores"
    players ||--o{ claims : "owns (mostly unknown)"
    players ||--o{ post_office : "vault"
    players ||--o{ shares : "holdings"
    players ||--o| share_payouts : "share dollars"
    players ||--o{ final_components : "final scoring"
    players ||--o| final_reported : "final total"

    games ||--o{ bank_slips : "per game"

    run_order ||--o| tickets : "pile position"
    run_order ||--o| employees : "pile position"
    run_order ||--o| events : "pile position"
    run_order ||--o| post_office : "pile position"
    run_order ||--o| story_cards : "pile position"

    card_ref ||--o| tickets : "canonical card"
    card_ref ||--{ card_ref_capacity : "per-color capacity"
    card_ref ||--o| ticket_postcards : "postcard mapping"

    tickets ||--{ ticket_punches : "who punched"
    postcard_ref ||--o| ticket_postcards : "punchable postcards"

    claims ||--{ claim_spots : "7 scratch spots"
    circus ||--{ circus_stickers : "stickers"

    cities ||--o{ routes : "endpoints"

    players {
        text color PK
        text company
        text gender
    }
    games {
        int year PK
        int seq
    }
    run_order {
        int id PK "run_id: unstack order"
        text card_type
    }
    card_ref {
        int ref_id PK
        text card_uid UK "EC-01.. synthesized"
        text card_code "printed code"
        text frontier
        text from_city
        text to_city
        int value
    }
    card_ref_capacity {
        int ref_id FK
        text color FK
        int capacity "1=postcard spot, 2=normal"
    }
    tickets {
        int ticket_id PK
        int run_id FK "NULL: vault or id collision"
        int ref_id FK
        text frontier "corrected"
        text frontier_original
        int value
        int active
        int postcard_card
    }
    ticket_punches {
        int ticket_id FK
        text color FK
        int punches
    }
    ticket_postcards {
        text card_uid PK
        int postcard_number
        text postcard_code "PC-xxx"
    }
    employees {
        int id PK
        int run_id FK
        text name
        int punches
        int active
        text origin
    }
    events {
        int id PK
        text name
        text origin
        int punched
        int active
    }
    post_office {
        int rowid_ PK
        int id FK "run_id; NULL = from vault"
        int card_number
        int read
        int punched
        text worth
        text vault FK
    }
    story_cards {
        int id PK
        text name
        int year "pause/stop markers"
        text location
    }
    claims {
        text card_code PK "CS-19..CS-24"
        text town
        text owner FK "only blue known"
    }
    claim_spots {
        text card_code FK
        int position
        int value "true value from reference"
        int revealed
    }
    circus {
        int rowid_ PK
        text player FK
        text type
        int valid
        int points
    }
    circus_stickers {
        int circus_row FK
        int position
        text color
    }
    timetable_cells {
        text player FK
        int row
        int col
        text city "NULL = not crossed off"
    }
    bank_slips {
        text player "incl 'missing' rows"
        int year FK
        int dollars
        int coins_in_hand
        int trains_bonus
        int ticket_value
        int miscalculation
    }
    postcard_ref {
        int card_number PK
        text title
        int punch_capacity
    }
    cities {
        text city PK
        text section "jigsaw piece"
        text company "ownership sticker"
        int port
    }
    routes {
        int route_id PK
        text section
        text city_a FK
        text city_b FK
        int length
        text track_color "NULL = unbuilt track bed"
        int bridges
    }
    shares {
        text company "share color incl white"
        text player FK
        int qty
    }
    share_bonus_ref {
        int rank PK
        int bonus "20/15/10/5"
    }
    share_payouts {
        text player PK
        int dollars "as tallied"
    }
    final_components {
        text player FK
        text component "bankslips/shares/circus/timetable/postcards"
        int dollars
    }
    final_reported {
        text player PK
        int dollars "final campaign score"
    }

title: Database schema
text:
Views







View
Purpose




v_frontier_counts
tickets tracked vs the canonical card list, per frontier


v_circus_scores
circus flyer points per player


v_timetable_scores
10 points per completed timetable row/column


v_claim_values
true totals vs revealed state per claim card


v_claim_earnings
dollars actually collected from claims; NULL when the card was fully scratched post-campaign


v_retirement_window
earliest/latest game year a card can have been retired, from its run position between dated story markers


v_ticket_retirement
the same, joined to retired tickets


v_ticket_rule_check
punch-capacity rule violations (empty = consistent)


v_city_degree
track connections per city (recreates the map sheet’s degree check)


v_share_check
share tally consistency: 10 per company, payouts exhaust the bonus pool


v_final_check
reported final breakdown vs independent derivations (all zeros = consistent)


v_final_standings
final placement by reported total


v_scores
bank slip scores per player per game


v_campaign_totals
campaign dollar totals per player

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Ticket to Ride (TtR) is Alan R. Moon’s classic gateway board game: a set-collection and network-building train game. Most players where I come from are familiar with Europe or Nordic Countries, but regardless of which TtR map you know, the core gameplay remains largely the same: collect coloured train cards, claim routes between cities, complete destination tickets for points, and score additional bonuses from map-specific objectives. The player with the most points at the end of the game wins.
Ticket to Ride Legacy: Legends of the West (2023) gives that familiar formula the legacy treatment pioneered by Rob Daviau and Matt Leacock in Pandemic Legacy (Seasons 1, 2 and 0 — in that order). Across a twelve-game campaign, the outcome of each session permanently changes future games. The map expands frontier by frontier, new cards and mechanics are introduced, existing components are modified with stickers or punches, and — unlike many legacy games — almost nothing is destroyed. Instead, components eventually retire to the Dead Letter Office, creating a persistent record of the campaign as the story unfolds.






WarningSpoiler warning



This report is one long spoiler. It contains the complete campaign, including every frontier, the fully revealed map, destination tickets, postcards, hidden card values, campaign events, and final scoring. If you think you might play TtR Legacy: Legends of the West in the future, stop reading now and come back after twelve games.

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Ticket to Ride (TtR) is Alan R. Moon’s classic gateway board game: a set-collection and network-building train game. Most players where I come from are familiar with Europe or Nordic Countries, but regardless of which TtR map you know, the core gameplay remains largely the same: collect coloured train cards, claim routes between cities, complete destination tickets for points, and score additional bonuses from map-specific objectives. The player with the most points at the end of the game wins.
Ticket to Ride Legacy: Legends of the West (2023) gives that familiar formula the legacy treatment pioneered by Rob Daviau and Matt Leacock in Pandemic Legacy (Seasons 1, 2 and 0 — in that order). Across a twelve-game campaign, the outcome of each session permanently changes future games. The map expands frontier by frontier, new cards and mechanics are introduced, existing components are modified with stickers or punches, and — unlike many legacy games — almost nothing is destroyed. Instead, components eventually retire to the Dead Letter Office, creating a persistent record of the campaign as the story unfolds.






WarningSpoiler warning



This report is one long spoiler. It contains the complete campaign, including every frontier, the fully revealed map, destination tickets, postcards, hidden card values, campaign events, and final scoring. If you think you might play TtR Legacy: Legends of the West in the future, stop reading now and come back after twelve games.

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Our campaign
Our campaign was played by five players. Throughout this report they are referred to by their player colours — Green, Black, Red, Yellow, and Blue. Since each player controls a railroad company throughout the campaign, the terms player and company are used interchangeably.
The game itself was a Christmas present from Green and Black to Black’s sisters, Red and Yellow, and Yellow’s husband, Blue. Everyone at the table was already well versed in TtR — particularly Europe — and are either budding hobbyists to hard-core gamers. Only Green and Black had previous experience with legacy games, having completed Pandemic Legacy Seasons 1 and 2 (with Season 0 still on the shelf of opportunity), and they also have some 20+ plays of Gloomhaven under their belt (still unfinished, but the dragon is nigh). As Pandemic Legacy and Gloomhaven are cooperative games in which players work together against the game, Legends of the West was everyone’s first competitive legacy campaign.
Although the campaign consists of only twelve games, ours spanned roughly two and a half years. The game was received as a Christmas present in 2023 and the first session took place in February 2024, but after several games the campaign paused from late 2024 until April 2026. In the meantime, life happened: pregnancies, the arrival of two new family members in 2025, and the usual challenge of finding time to get everyone back around the table.
After the campaign concluded, the complete game state was painstakingly reconstructed by hand. Every remaining card in the Storeroom, Post Office, Company Vaults, and the entire Dead Letter Office was catalogued card by card and entered into the database on which the analyses in this report are based.

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Campaign basics
Legends of the West is played over twelve games spanning the years 1865 to 1898, with each session advancing the calendar by three years (i.e., 1865:3:1898). Every player controls a railroad company whose progress persists throughout the campaign. Between games, the map expands westward through newly opened Frontiers (jigsaw pieces that gradually form the board), while new cards, mechanics, and scoring opportunities themed to each frontier are introduced. As a result, every campaign unfolds differently, as the order in which these mechanics appear depends on the sequence in which the frontiers are explored.
Unlike a traditional game of TtR, companies develop asymmetrically over time. Employees provide unique abilities for a single game (my personal favourite is the Financier). The game is seeded with Big Cities (with more added during the campaign); every time a company builds a route into a Big City, it draws one bonus train card from the face-down deck (but only one bonus card per turn — even if a single route connects two Big Cities at once). Company Headquarters — permanent stickers placed on small city markers — create ongoing sources of income for their owners. The campaign’s recurring villain, Mama O’Connell, has a habit of collecting money that would otherwise be yours: she can establish headquarters of her own, and she frequently appears in the event deck demanding payment, while other events may reward companies that meet certain conditions. Components, primarily cards, are never destroyed. Instead, they may be modified and stored for future games until they are eventually retired to the Dead Letter Office — or, in some cases, simply depleted, such as a fully used sticker sheet.
Unlike traditional TtR, Legends of the West tracks dollars rather than victory points. Money is earned from completed destination tickets, coins remaining in hand, bonuses for trains left at the end of the game, favourable events, and the $2 toll other companies pay when building into one of your headquarters cities. Money is lost through failed destination tickets, unfavourable events, payments claimed by Mama O’Connell, and payments claimed by other companies (note you can also pay these tolls with train cards instead). Every game begins with each company holding $5 (although employee abilities may increase this starting amount).






NoteWhy 1865–1898?





The campaign follows the great American railroad boom. It begins in 1865, the year the American Civil War ended, and concludes in 1898 as the nineteenth century — and the era of westward railroad expansion — draws to a close. The rulebook deliberately leans into familiar themes of frontier expansion and the Wild West, while also acknowledging that this history came at a significant human cost through the dispossession of Native American peoples and the exploitation of railroad labourers. It is worth keeping this broader historical context in mind while enjoying the game’s lighter treatment of the period.

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Score progression
When the campaign starts, the board is minimal and a game is relatively quick (less than an hour) — and it is hard to tell what a “good” score even looks like. What we noticed is that as the campaign progressed, the scores got higher. The more valuable tickets would be sought after and depleted: one feature of the legacy system that keeps the ticket deck from bloating is that whenever a company completes a ticket, it punches the card — some tickets allow one punch, others two (more on that later) — and once full, the ticket is retired to the Dead Letter Office.


Code
missing_scores <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT year, dollars FROM bank_slips WHERE player = 'missing'
") |>
  mutate(player = "(missing)")

pg_levels <- c(names(player_colors), "(missing)")
pg_colors <- c(player_colors, "(missing)" = "gray45")
pg_lines <- setNames(c(rep("solid", 5), "dashed"), pg_levels)

per_game <- bind_rows(
  mutate(scores, player = as.character(player)),
  missing_scores
) |>
  mutate(player = factor(player, levels = pg_levels))

ggplot(per_game, aes(year, dollars, color = player, linetype = player)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = filter(per_game, player != "(missing)"), size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = pg_colors) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = pg_lines) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(scores$year)) +
  labs(
    title = "Dollars per game",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars", color = NULL, linetype = NULL
  )





Dollars earned by each company throughout the campaign. The dashed grey line indicates the recommended dollar amount awarded to a player who missed that particular game, as specified by the end-of-campaign story deck card.




One feature of the campaign deserves a confession. The rulebook is initially very vague about absent players: it merely says the campaign can continue even when not everyone is present. We took that to mean a missing player would simply receive nothing — hence the scheduling nightmare and the long hiatus. Only at the end of the campaign does a story deck card make up for those missed games, awarding a recommended dollar amount for each game a player sat out. Rather than attempting to recreate the absent player’s result, the designers simply award a fixed amount per game of the campaign — presumably chosen to approximate what a typical company would have earned during that game.
Because our campaign contains complete results for all five companies, we can evaluate how well these recommendations match observed play. Are they an unbiased estimate of an average player’s earnings? Do they systematically overestimate or underestimate actual performance? And would following the rule have unfairly advantaged or disadvantaged a player over the course of the campaign?
The remainder of this section compares the official “missing player” values against the observed campaign results — effectively an evaluation of one of the game’s hidden balancing mechanisms.

Game-level comparison


Code
official <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT year, dollars AS official FROM bank_slips WHERE player = 'missing'
")

obs <- scores |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    mean = mean(dollars), median = median(dollars),
    sd = sd(dollars), .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(official, by = "year") |>
  mutate(diff = mean - official)

knitr::kable(
  obs |> mutate(across(c(mean, median, sd, diff), ~ round(.x, 1))) |>
    select(year, official, mean, median, sd, `mean − official` = diff),
  caption = "Official missing-player amount vs observed earnings per game"
)

Code
player_official <- scores |>
  left_join(official, by = "year") |>
  mutate(resid = dollars - official)

me   <- mean(player_official$resid)
mae  <- mean(abs(player_official$resid))
rmse <- sqrt(mean(player_official$resid^2))


Treating the guideline as a prediction of each company’s earnings in each game (60 observations), its mean error is $11.6 (positive = players actually earned more), with a mean absolute error of $23 and RMSE of $34.2. The bias is not constant, though — it grows with the campaign economy:


Code
ggplot(player_official, aes(year, resid, color = player)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray55", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = player_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(scores$year)) +
  labs(
    title = "Actual minus official, per game",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars above the guideline", color = NULL
  )





Residuals (actual − official) per game. The guideline tracks the early campaign well, then falls increasingly behind the inflating economy.






Per-company performance


Code
ggplot(player_official, aes(player, resid, fill = player)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray55", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = player_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Actual minus official, by company",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars above the guideline"
  )





Distribution of (actual − official) across the 12 games. Boxes above zero mean the company out-earned the missing-player guideline.






Code
tests <- player_official |>
  group_by(player) |>
  summarise(
    avg_dollars = round(mean(dollars), 1),
    avg_diff = round(mean(resid), 1),
    sd_diff = round(sd(resid), 1),
    p_wilcoxon = signif(
      wilcox.test(dollars, official, paired = TRUE, exact = FALSE)$p.value, 2
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

knitr::kable(
  tests,
  col.names = c("company", "avg dollars", "avg − official",
                "sd", "Wilcoxon p"),
  caption = "Per-company deviation from the guideline
    (Wilcoxon signed-rank, paired, n = 12)"
)



Per-company deviation from the guideline (Wilcoxon signed-rank, paired, n = 12)

Interpretation
If the recommendations closely matched observed play, they would offer a fair way to handle missed sessions. The data says they are increasingly stingy: the guideline is roughly calibrated for the first half of the campaign, but from 1883 onward the table’s economy inflates (bigger tickets, headquarters tolls, richer events) while the guideline grows only linearly. Averaged over the whole campaign, four of the five companies out-earned the guideline by $13–19 per game; only Blue essentially broke even against it (average difference $-1.4). With just 12 paired observations per company, none of the Wilcoxon tests reach conventional significance (Green comes closest at p = 0.074), so the per-company differences should be read as descriptive. Still, the direction is consistent: skipping a late-campaign game and taking the official payout would have been a bad trade for everyone except perhaps Blue.
The campaign total is what decides the winner — the running sum of the bank slips through 1898, then each post-campaign addition stepped in one at a time: post cards (from the vaults), circus flyer, shares, and finally the timetable. The number at the right is each company’s final score.


Code
cum_scores <- scores |>
  arrange(player, year) |>
  group_by(player) |>
  mutate(total = cumsum(dollars)) |>
  ungroup()

component_order <- c("postcards", "circus", "shares", "timetable")
component_x <- setNames(1901 + 3 * seq_along(component_order), component_order)

additions_wide <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT player, component, dollars FROM final_components
  WHERE component <> 'bankslips'
")

steps <- cum_scores |>
  filter(year == max(year)) |>
  select(player, total)
step_rows <- list()
for (comp in component_order) {
  steps <- steps |>
    left_join(filter(additions_wide, component == comp) |>
        select(player, add = dollars), by = "player") |>
    mutate(total = total + coalesce(add, 0)) |>
    select(-add)
  step_rows[[comp]] <- mutate(steps, year = component_x[[comp]])
}

cum_full <- bind_rows(
  select(cum_scores, player, year, total),
  bind_rows(step_rows)
) |>
  mutate(player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)))

totals_lab <- cum_full |>
  filter(year == max(year)) |>
  arrange(total) |>
  mutate(
    lab_y = total,
    # push overlapping labels apart (min 45 dollar-units of separation)
    lab_y = Reduce(
      function(prev, cur) max(cur, prev + 45),
      lab_y, accumulate = TRUE
    )
  )

ggplot(cum_full, aes(year, total, color = player)) +
  annotate("segment",
    x = 1865, xend = 1898, y = -95, yend = -95,
    color = "gray55", linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  annotate("text",
    x = (1865 + 1898) / 2, y = -160, label = "bank slips",
    color = "gray45", size = 3.2
  ) +
  annotate("segment",
    x = min(component_x), xend = max(component_x), y = -95, yend = -95,
    color = "gray55", linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  annotate("text",
    x = mean(range(component_x)), y = -160, label = "vault",
    color = "gray45", size = 3.2
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1901, color = "gray80", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text",
    x = 1900, y = 650, label = "post campaign",
    angle = 90, color = "gray55", size = 3
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_text(
    data = totals_lab,
    aes(y = lab_y, label = paste0("$", total)),
    hjust = -0.2, size = 3.3, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = player_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(unique(scores$year), component_x),
    labels = c(unique(scores$year), "post cards", "circus flyer",
               "shares", "timetable"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.06))
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 20, 26, 10)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Campaign total (bank slips, then each addition stepped in)",
    x = NULL, y = "cumulative dollars", color = NULL
  )









The post-campaign vault additions nearly rewrote the podium: green’s $259 of additions ($110 shares, $71 circus, $30 timetable, $48 post cards) closed the gap to black from $66 to just $18 — final score $1363 to $1345.


Code
additions <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT player, component, dollars
  FROM final_components
  WHERE component <> 'bankslips'
") |>
  mutate(
    player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)),
    component = factor(component,
      levels = c("shares", "circus", "timetable", "postcards")
    )
  )

ggplot(additions, aes(player, dollars, fill = component)) +
  geom_col(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    shares = "#4477aa", circus = "#ee6677",
    timetable = "#228833", postcards = "#ccbb44"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Post-campaign vault additions by component",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars", fill = NULL
  )











Did the ordering change?
Rank 1 = leading. First the rank within each individual game — extended past 1898 with the rank within each vault addition (who earned the most post cards, circus flyer, shares, timetable) and the final total. Then the rank on the running campaign total as each of those steps in — the race that matters.


Code
ranks <- cum_scores |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(
    game_rank = rank(-dollars, ties.method = "min"),
    campaign_rank = rank(-total, ties.method = "min")
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# per-component ranks: who earned the most from each vault addition,
# then the final standings as 'total'
comp_ranks <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT player, component, dollars FROM final_components
  WHERE component <> 'bankslips'
") |>
  group_by(component) |>
  mutate(rank = rank(-dollars, ties.method = "min")) |>
  ungroup() |>
  transmute(player, year = rank_step_x[component], rank)

total_rank <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT player, place FROM v_final_standings") |>
  transmute(player, year = rank_step_x[["total"]], rank = place)

game_ranks_full <- bind_rows(
  transmute(ranks, player = as.character(player), year, rank = game_rank),
  comp_ranks,
  total_rank
) |>
  mutate(player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)))

plot_bump(
  game_ranks_full,
  "Rank within each game, each vault addition, and the final total",
  rank_breaks, rank_labels
)











Code
# cumulative rank at every year and after each vault addition steps in
cum_ranks_full <- cum_full |>
  bind_rows(
    cum_full |>
      filter(year == max(year)) |>
      mutate(year = rank_step_x[["total"]])
  ) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(rank = rank(-total, ties.method = "min")) |>
  ungroup() |>
  transmute(player, year, rank)

plot_bump(
  cum_ranks_full,
  "Rank on running total (bank slips, then each vault addition)",
  rank_breaks, rank_labels
)

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
section: Where do the points come from?
text:
Where do the points come from?
Bank slips break each game’s score into components: coins in hand, bonus for trains remaining, and completed-ticket value.


Code
components <- scores |>
  select(player, year, coins_in_hand, trains_bonus, ticket_value) |>
  pivot_longer(-c(player, year), names_to = "component", values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(component = recode(component,
    coins_in_hand = "coins in hand",
    trains_bonus = "trains remaining bonus",
    ticket_value = "ticket value"
  ))

component_totals <- components |>
  group_by(player, component) |>
  summarise(total = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(component_totals, aes(player, total, fill = player)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = player_colors) +
  facet_wrap(~component) +
  labs(
    title = "Campaign totals by scoring component",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars"
  )











Code
ggplot(components, aes(year, value, color = player)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = player_colors) +
  facet_wrap(~component, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(scores$year)) +
  labs(
    title = "Scoring components over time",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars", color = NULL
  )









The trains-remaining bonus can be read backwards: the bank-slip schedule pays $16/12/9/7/6 for 0–4 trains left and $4/2/0 for 5–7, 8–10, 11+ — so the bonus reveals how many trains each company had left on the table:


Code
trains <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM v_trains_remaining") |>
  mutate(
    player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)),
    trains_plot = case_when(
      is.na(trains_min)          ~ NA_real_,
      is.na(trains_max)          ~ 11,
      TRUE                       ~ (trains_min + trains_max) / 2
    )
  )

ggplot(trains, aes(year, trains_plot, color = player)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = player_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(scores$year)) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = c(0:4, 6, 9, 11),
    labels = c("0", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5-7", "8-10", "11+")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Trains remaining at game end (derived from the bonus)",
    x = NULL, y = "trains remaining", color = NULL
  )









One bank slip needed an explanation: yellow’s 1883 bonus of $28 is off the schedule — a postcard effect that game paid $28 instead of the usual $16 for 0 trains, so it plots as 0 trains remaining.

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Tickets
Punches per player — how many ticket completions each company recorded over the whole campaign, split by normal tickets and postcard tickets (single-punch tickets that retire immediately and earn a post card).


Code
punches <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT p.color AS player, c.capacity, SUM(p.punches) AS punches
  FROM ticket_punches p
  JOIN tickets t ON t.ticket_id = p.ticket_id
  JOIN card_ref_capacity c ON c.ref_id = t.ref_id AND c.color = p.color
  GROUP BY p.color, c.capacity
") |>
  mutate(
    player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)),
    type = ifelse(capacity == 1, "postcard tickets", "normal tickets")
  )

ggplot(punches, aes(player, punches, fill = player)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = punches),
    vjust = -0.4, size = 3, color = "gray30"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = player_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.09))) +
  facet_wrap(~type) +
  labs(
    title = "Ticket punches per company",
    x = NULL, y = "punches"
  )









Ticket dollars actually collected (ticket value × punches):


Code
ticket_dollars <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT p.color AS player, SUM(p.punches * t.value) AS dollars
  FROM ticket_punches p
  JOIN tickets t ON t.ticket_id = p.ticket_id
  GROUP BY p.color
") |>
  mutate(player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)))

ggplot(ticket_dollars, aes(player, dollars, fill = player)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = player_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Total dollars from completed tickets",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars"
  )

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Claims
The endgame scratch cards. On partially-scratched cards the scratched spots are exactly the claims collected during play; fully-scratched cards were opened out of curiosity afterwards, so their in-play earnings are unknown.


Code
claims <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM v_claim_earnings")
knitr::kable(
  claims |>
    mutate(
      earned = ifelse(earnings_known == 1, earned_dollars, "unknown (fully scratched)"),
      owner = coalesce(owner, note, "?")
    ) |>
    select(card = card_code, town, owner, earned),
  caption = "Claim card earnings (all cards hide the same $98 total but in random ordering)"
)



Claim card earnings (all cards hide the same $98 total but in random ordering)








card
town
owner
earned




CS-19
Vancouver
one of black/green/yellow/red
41


CS-20
Portland
no one (extra card)
0


CS-21
Spokane
one of black/green/yellow/red
55


CS-22
Calgary
one of black/green/yellow/red
unknown (fully scratched)


CS-23
Seattle
one of black/green/yellow/red
62


CS-24
Helena
blue
unknown (fully scratched)

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Company shares
Sixty shares across six railway companies (including the neutral white railway), ten per company. Most shares in a company ranks first (at least one share required to qualify; ties broken by the lowest-numbered share held), paying $20/15/10/5 down the ranks.


Code
company_colors <- c(player_colors, white = "#9e9e9e", total = "#555555")

shares <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM shares")

share_totals <- shares |>
  group_by(player) |>
  summarise(qty = sum(qty), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(company = "total")

shares_plot <- bind_rows(shares, share_totals) |>
  group_by(company) |>
  mutate(alpha_val = ifelse(qty == 0, 0, 0.15 + 0.85 * qty / max(qty))) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)),
    company = factor(company,
      levels = rev(c("black", "blue", "green", "yellow", "red", "white", "total"))
    )
  )

ggplot(shares_plot, aes(player, company)) +
  geom_tile(aes(fill = company, alpha = alpha_val),
    color = "white", linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(qty > 0, qty, ""),
        color = alpha_val > 0.6),
    size = 3.6, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = company_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_alpha_identity() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "white", `FALSE` = "gray25"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.6) +
  labs(
    title = "Share holdings",
    caption = "Each company has 10 shares.",
    x = "players", y = "company"
  )











Code
payouts <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM share_payouts") |>
  mutate(player = factor(player, levels = names(player_colors)))

ggplot(payouts, aes(player, dollars, fill = player)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollars), vjust = -0.4, size = 3, color = "gray30") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = player_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.12))) +
  labs(
    title = "Share dollars ($295 paid out in total)",
    x = NULL, y = "dollars"
  )









Green’s diversified portfolio (22 of 60 shares, holding stakes in every company) pays $110 — more than a third of the entire pool.

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
The board
The game board as a network: 200 tracks connecting 63 cities across 12 jigsaw sections. Fictional cities (Nuevos Angeles, Cemetery City, Pacific Haven, Baja) sit at plausible approximate positions; dashed gray = unbuilt track beds. The cities are colored by the sticker locations of our campaign — your board may vary.


Code
coords <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT cc.*, c.company, c.port
  FROM city_coords cc JOIN cities c ON c.city = cc.city
") |>
  mutate(
    port_type = ifelse(coalesce(port, 0) == 1, "port", "inland"),
    company = ifelse(is.na(company), NA, company)
  )
routes <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT r.*, a.lat AS lat_a, a.lon AS lon_a, b.lat AS lat_b, b.lon AS lon_b
  FROM routes r
  JOIN city_coords a ON a.city = r.city_a
  JOIN city_coords b ON b.city = r.city_b
")

track_palette <- c(
  black = "#1a1a1a", blue = "#1f77c4", green = "#2a9d3a",
  yellow = "#e6b400", red = "#d62728", white = "#c8c8c8",
  gray = "#8a8a8a"
)

sticker_colors <- c(
  player_colors,
  "Mama O'Connel" = "#8B5A2B",   # brown
  big = "#9467bd"                 # purple
)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = filter(routes, is.na(track_color)),
    aes(x = lon_a, y = lat_a, xend = lon_b, yend = lat_b),
    color = "gray70", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = filter(routes, !is.na(track_color)),
    aes(x = lon_a, y = lat_a, xend = lon_b, yend = lat_b,
        color = track_color, linewidth = length),
    alpha = 0.55
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = coords,
    aes(lon, lat, fill = company, shape = port_type),
    size = 2.6, color = "gray20", stroke = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = coords, aes(lon, lat, label = city),
    size = 2.4, vjust = -1.0, check_overlap = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = track_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = sticker_colors, na.value = NA,
    name = "company sticker",
    guide = guide_legend(override.aes = list(shape = 21, size = 3))
  ) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(inland = 21, port = 24),
    name = NULL,
    guide = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 3))
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_continuous(range = c(0.3, 1.8), guide = "none") +
  coord_quickmap() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(title = "The board: tracks as printed, cities by company sticker (triangles = ports)")










Opening the frontiers
How the map grows (per the official rules): at the end of a game, the winner chooses which jigsaw piece to add next — its cities and tickets come into play from the next game on. The dead letter office remembers the order: frontier cards sit between dated story markers in the pile, giving both the sequence and (with the one-frontier-per-game constraint) the year each section became playable. Drag the slider (or press play) to watch the board unfold from the initial East Coast start area; cities are colored by jigsaw section, and tracks appear once both their endpoints are unlocked.


Code
frontier_map <- c(
  "great plains" = "Great Plains", "florida" = "Florida",
  "sierra madre" = "Sierra Madre", "haunted waste" = "Haunted Wastes",
  "badlands" = "Badlands", "california" = "California",
  "open range" = "Open Range", "cascadia" = "Cascadia"
)

unlocks <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT s.id, s.name, w.latest_year
  FROM story_cards s
  JOIN v_retirement_window w ON w.run_id = s.id
") |>
  mutate(key = tolower(name)) |>
  filter(key %in% names(frontier_map)) |>
  arrange(desc(id)) |>   # deeper in the pile = chosen earlier
  transmute(section = unname(frontier_map[key]), year = latest_year) |>
  # a frontier is chosen at the end of one game and playable from the
  # next, so in-play years must strictly increase (one per game); this
  # also corrects two markers that sit scrambled in the pile
  mutate(year = Reduce(
    function(prev, cur) max(cur, prev + 3),
    year, accumulate = TRUE
  ))

initial_sections <- c("Northeast", "Atlantic Coast", "The South", "Appalachia")

city_sections <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT c.city, c.section, cc.lat, cc.lon FROM cities c
  JOIN city_coords cc ON cc.city = c.city
")

# cumulative frames: each step adds one frontier's sections
acc <- initial_sections
frame_defs <- list(tibble(frame = "1865 · Initial", section = acc))
for (i in seq_len(nrow(unlocks))) {
  acc <- c(acc, unlocks$section[i])
  frame_defs[[i + 1]] <- tibble(
    frame = paste0(unlocks$year[i], " · ", unlocks$section[i]),
    section = acc
  )
}
frames_df <- bind_rows(frame_defs)

cities_anim <- frames_df |>
  inner_join(city_sections, by = "section",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  ) |>
  mutate(tooltip = paste0("<b>", city, "</b><br>", section))

routes_anim <- frames_df |>
  distinct(frame) |>
  cross_join(routes) |>
  semi_join(
    cities_anim |> select(frame, city),
    by = c("frame", "city_a" = "city")
  ) |>
  semi_join(
    cities_anim |> select(frame, city),
    by = c("frame", "city_b" = "city")
  )

p_unlock <- ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = routes_anim,
    aes(x = lon_a, y = lat_a, xend = lon_b, yend = lat_b, frame = frame),
    color = "gray80", linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = cities_anim,
    aes(lon, lat, color = section, frame = frame, text = tooltip),
    size = 2.4
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1.25) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "How the board unfolded", color = "jigsaw section")

ggplotly(p_unlock, tooltip = "text") |>
  animation_opts(frame = 1200, transition = 300, redraw = FALSE) |>
  animation_slider(currentvalue = list(prefix = "unlocked: ", font = list(size = 14))) |>
  config(displayModeBar = FALSE)








Hotspots
Which cities did completed tickets actually connect? Each punch on a ticket counts once for each of its two endpoint cities. Hover a city for its name, punch count, and most frequent visitor — the company that punched the most tickets through it.


Code
city_players <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  WITH endpoint_punches AS (
    SELECT t.from_city AS city, p.color AS player, p.punches FROM tickets t
    JOIN ticket_punches p ON p.ticket_id = t.ticket_id
    UNION ALL
    SELECT t.to_city, p.color, p.punches FROM tickets t
    JOIN ticket_punches p ON p.ticket_id = t.ticket_id
  )
  SELECT city, player, SUM(punches) AS punches
  FROM endpoint_punches GROUP BY city, player
")

top_visitors <- city_players |>
  filter(punches > 0) |>
  group_by(city) |>
  filter(punches == max(punches)) |>
  summarise(
    top_label = paste0(paste(player, collapse = " & "),
                       " (", first(punches), ")"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

hot <- city_players |>
  group_by(city) |>
  summarise(punches = sum(punches), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(top_visitors, by = "city") |>
  inner_join(coords, by = "city") |>
  mutate(tooltip = paste0(
    "<b>", city, "</b><br>punches: ", punches,
    "<br>most frequent visitor: ", coalesce(top_label, "none")
  ))

p_hot <- ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = routes,
    aes(x = lon_a, y = lat_a, xend = lon_b, yend = lat_b),
    color = "gray85", linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = hot,
    aes(lon, lat, size = punches, color = punches, text = tooltip),
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 9)) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "inferno", direction = -1) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1.25) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(title = "Ticket hotspots: endpoint cities weighted by punches")

ggplotly(p_hot, tooltip = "text") |>
  config(displayModeBar = FALSE)








When was the network built?
Retired tickets sit between dated story markers in the dead letter office, which bounds when they were completed. Using the midpoint of each ticket’s retirement window as a rough timestamp:


Code
era_tickets <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT t.from_city, t.to_city,
         (COALESCE(w.earliest_year, 1865) + COALESCE(w.latest_year, 1901)) / 2 AS mid_year,
         SUM(p.punches) AS punches
  FROM tickets t
  JOIN v_retirement_window w ON w.run_id = t.run_id
  JOIN ticket_punches p ON p.ticket_id = t.ticket_id
  WHERE t.active = 0
  GROUP BY t.ticket_id
  HAVING punches > 0
") |>
  mutate(era = cut(mid_year,
    breaks = c(1864, 1874, 1886, 1901),
    labels = c("early (1865-1874)", "mid (1877-1886)", "late (1889-1901)")
  )) |>
  inner_join(coords, by = c("from_city" = "city")) |>
  rename(lat_a = lat, lon_a = lon) |>
  inner_join(coords, by = c("to_city" = "city")) |>
  rename(lat_b = lat, lon_b = lon)

ggplot(era_tickets) +
  geom_segment(
    data = routes,
    aes(x = lon_a, y = lat_a, xend = lon_b, yend = lat_b),
    color = "gray88", linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = lon_a, y = lat_a, xend = lon_b, yend = lat_b,
        linewidth = punches),
    color = "#c1440e", alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_continuous(range = c(0.3, 1.6), guide = "none") +
  coord_quickmap() +
  facet_wrap(~era) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title = "Completed ticket connections by campaign era",
    subtitle = "Straight lines connect ticket endpoints (not actual track paths)"
  )

title: Ticket to Ride Legacy — Data Dive
text:
Next up

Route the ticket connections along actual board tracks (shortest path over the routes network) instead of straight lines.
Claim / postcard geography: where the postcard tickets cluster.
